data.title: US retailers fall; China retaliates with 34% import tariffs
data.description:
** Shares of U.S. retailers fall in early trade

** China retaliates with 34% import tariffs on all U.S. goods in response to "Liberation Day" levies imposed by President Donald Trump on Wednesday

** New tariffs announced by Trump on countries in Asia include Vietnam, Indonesia and China, which are key manufacturing hubs for products such as apparel, home goods and electronics

** Shares of big-box retailers Walmart and Target down about 2% each

** Shares of home improvement retailers Home Depot and Lowe's down 0.7% each, while online furniture retailer Wayfair down 9.8%

** Shares of toymakers Mattel down 4.1%, while Hasbro down 5%

** Shares of department stores Kohl's and Macy's down 6% and 3%, respectively

** Shares of electronics retailer Best Buy down 5.3%

** Shares of Dollar Tree down 3%

** S&P 500 index down 3.2%, while S&P 500 Consumer Staples index down 0.4%, and S&P 500 Consumer Discretionary index down 3.3%

data.title: Semiconductor Sector Under Pressure After China Counters in Trump's Tariff WarAnalysis
data.description:
By Dean Seal

Shares of Nvidia and other semiconductor companies slid early Friday amid a flurry of tariffs between the U.S. and Asian countries that could subdue electronics demand and hinder Big Tech's artificial-intelligence infrastructure buildout.

"The AI revolution trade would be significantly slowed down by these head scratching tariffs that need to be negotiated to realistic levels," Wedbush analysts said in a research note.

Nvidia's stock was down 4.1% at $97.65 in early trading. Shares of Broadcom were down 6.3% at $144.12. Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing's American depositary receipts were down 4.8% at $149.79. Shares of Qualcomm were down 4.2% at $133.64 while shares of Intel dropped 6.7% to $20.93.

President Trump has issued a spate of tariffs against its main trading partners with the stated goal of bringing manufacturing back to the U.S. That includes a 34% tariff on Chinese imports and a 32% tariff on imports from Taiwan. China has fired back with its own matching 34% levy on goods imported from the U.S.

The trade war could hamstring the technology industry's billion-dollar efforts so far to establish AI infrastructure in the U.S., Oppenheimer analysts said in a research note. Ironically, that would undermine one of the Trump administration's own stated goals, they said.

While Trump hasn't issued direct tariffs on semiconductor companies, direct chip exports to the U.S. are small, Mizuho analysts said in a research note. Most are exported to China, Mexico, Vietnam or Malaysia, where they are assembled into servers and computer equipment that may be subject to tariffs when they are then shipped to the U.S., the analysts said.

"We see potential for knock-on collateral impacts if servers [and] subsystems imported potentially come under the tariff umbrella," the Mizuho analysts said.

The stock declines come after executives at semiconductor companies have looked to downplay the potential impact of tariffs.

Nvidia Chief Executive Jensen Huang said at the company's developer conference in March that if new tariffs push the U.S. into a recession, tech companies would shift even more investment toward the AI buildout because of its growth potential. Nvidia is also moving more of its manufacturing to the U.S. to accommodate the duties, he said.

"Tariffs will have a little impact for us short term," the CEO said. "Long term, we're going to have manufacturing onshore."

But a drop in demand for electronics and Big Tech spending could effectively push the U.S. technology sector back by a decade while China steamrolls ahead, according to Wedbush analysts. The current levies would lead to a shut-off valve for spending by U.S. tech companies, they said.

Write to Dean Seal at dean.seal@wsj.com

data.title: ICE Canola Erases Thursday Gains
data.description:
WINNIPEG, Manitoba--The ICE Futures canola market was weaker Friday morning as global markets continued to struggle under the new tariff scheme unveiled by U.S. President Trump on Wednesday.

Chicago soyoil, European rapeseed and Malaysian palm oil were seeing sharp losses for a second straight day. Meanwhile, crude oil lost approximately US$5 per barrel.

The Canadian dollar was down nearly two-thirds of a U.S. cent compared with Thursday's close.

The Canadian Grain Commission reported 233,000 metric tons of canola were shipped for export during the week ended March 30, more than twice than the 113,800 reported the week before. So far this marketing year, 6.866 million metric tons were shipped, compared with 4.031 million one year earlier.

Nearly 27,000 contracts were traded. Prices in Canadian dollars per metric ton as of 8:38 a.m. CDT:

data.title: Canada Unemployment Rate to Reach 8% By Mid-Year on Trade FalloutMarket Talk
data.description: Oxford Economics expects Canada's unemployment rate to climb to 8% by mid-year, as the economy struggles to deal with hefty US tariffs on Canadian-made autos, steel, aluminum and other products deemed to be noncompliant with USMCA. Job losses in March, a net 32,600, were broad-based, and the headline number was buffered by a rise in the self-employed category. Despite this dour labor-market outlook, Oxford believes the Bank of Canada will keep its benchmark interest rate unchanged on April 16, at 2.75%, as officials try to balance the risk of tariffs fueling broad price increases with pending job losses. (Paul.Vieira@wsj.com, @paulvieira)

data.title: Ibovespa Poised for Sharp Weekly Losses
data.description:
The Ibovespa plunged more than 2.5% to fall below the 128,000 level on Friday, setting the stage for a near 3% weekly loss as escalating trade tensions disrupted global markets.

U.S. tariffs, compounded by China’s retaliatory 34% levy on American goods, have forced investors into risk-off positions.

Major sectors, particularly energy and commodities, have suffered markedly with key players such as Petrobras and Vale declining over 4% and 3%, respectively, amid plummeting oil prices and a broader contraction in commodity demand.

Domestically, uncertainty over anticipated rate cuts by the Fed and ECB amid recession fears—coupled with geopolitical tensions and mixed signals from Brazilian trade policies—has further dampened market activity, leaving most equities trading sharply lower as investors brace for additional trade war uncertainties that threaten global growth and demand.

data.title: US asset managers fall after China's retaliatory tariffs extend market downturn
data.description:
** Shares of U.S. asset managers fall in early trading after China's finance ministry said on Friday it will respond to President Donald Trump's tariffs with additional tariffs of 34% on all U.S. goods from April 10

** Apollo APO.N down 8.4%, Blackstone BX.N and BlackRock BLK.N drop 6%, and Charles Schwab SCHW.N slips 4.5%

** Janus Henderson falls 6.8% and AllianceBernstein falls 1.9%

** Asset managers could see a drop in fees, which is often tied to the value of assets under management, if trade war fears, economic uncertainty and inflationary pressures from tariffs cause a prolonged downturn in the markets

** "After a strong finish to 2024, the escalating trade war and federal spending cuts have increased the risk of stagflation," BofA analysts said in a note

** Brokerage said that management fees will be up in Q1 2025 since correction occurred in month 3 of the quarter, but also flagged that fees should be down in Q2 if markets don't rebound

data.title: Swiss Re’s Crosby: Redefining peak perils, accumulation and prevention three key issues in Canada
data.description:
(The Insurer) - Redefining what peak perils are, getting a better understanding of accumulation risk, and prevention and mitigation efforts are three of the key issues facing Canada’s insurance market, Swiss Re executive Jolee Crosby has declared.

Speaking at Swiss Re’s 39th annual Canadian Insurance Outlook Breakfast on Thursday in Toronto, Crosby, who serves as CEO of reinsurance for Canada and the English Caribbean, said the industry needs to reconsider the impact of so-called secondary perils.

As Crosby highlighted, Canada was hit by record catastrophe losses in 2024 – estimated at more than C$8.5 billion ($6 billion) by CatIQ.

The bulk of that total came from four secondary peril events: a hailstorm in Calgary, the remnants of Hurricane Debby, a wildfire in Jasper, and severe flooding in southern Ontario.

“When I first came to Canada, and I was looking at the historical loss trends, if you’d told me you would have had an C$8 billion or C$9 billion year last year, I would say, ‘no way’.

“Because if you look at the history, while it has been going up, it has now gone to another level. And over the last three years, a C$1 billion event has become normal.”

Crosby highlighted that this trend is being seen worldwide too, with multiple smaller secondary peril events accounting for a larger share of losses.

“Now you have multiple $1 billion or $2 billion events that, from an accumulation perspective, is still the same amount as one large event.

“So we have to think differently about how peak perils are defined, because it's only through that that we can adjust and create a stronger, resilient insurance ecosystem for society.”

With increased urbanisation, Crosby said “it’s very important” the market gets a better understanding of accumulation risk otherwise it will continue to be surprised by bigger-than-expected loss events.

“Understanding all of that means that we can, again, have a stronger ecosystem,” she said.

The third key issue impacting the industry, Crosby highlighted, is around risk prevention.

“Prevention really reaps benefits so much when the event occurs,” Crosby stated.

“There’s a lot of good work going on at many of the individual (insurance) companies in terms of build-back-better prevention programmes, and we just need to continue to work on those,” she said.

data.title: NioCorp to Develop Critical Minerals Project in Nebraska
data.description:
NioCorp Developments said Friday it is developing a critical minerals project in Nebraska with an aim to produce the processed rare earths that China plans to limit US access to as part of its retaliatory action against the Trump administration's latest tariffs.

Chinese Ministry of Commerce had said it was restricting the exports of certain rare earth elements, including samarium and scandium. The restrictions include metals, alloys, oxides, compounds, and related products of the rare earths.

The new project in Nebraska is also expected to produce niobium and titanium, the company said.

NioCorp shares were up more than 4% in recent Friday trading.

data.title: Carrefour Brasil soars as French retailer hikes offer to take it private
data.description:
** Shares in Carrefour Brasil soar after France's Carrefour SA hiked its offer to take the Brazilian unit private

** Carrefour said it would pay 8.50 reais per share of the Brazilian unit, formally known as Atacadao SA, up from a previous offer of 7.70 reais

** Carrefour, which owns about 67% of the Brazilian unit, first proposed to buy the remaining shares in February, saying it would allow it to manage operations with more agility

** Santander analysts say the latest move is yet another positive development for minority shareholders in Carrefour Brasil

** "It not only shows Carrefour SA's willingness to close the deal, but it also indicates some bargaining power for minority shareholders which seemed to be limited at first," the analysts say

** Carrefour Brasil's shares rise more than 11% to 8.25 reais each in early Sao Paulo trading

** The retailer is the biggest gainer on benchmark stock index Bovespa , which has fallen more than 2.5%

data.title: EHang Says US-China Tariff Actions Won't Impact Operations
data.description:
EHang said Friday it doesn't expect the recent tariff developments between the US and China to materially impact its operations as its "secure and independent" supply chain ensures no disruption from policy changes.

The company said it currently doesn't export its autonomous aerial vehicles or related products to the US, nor does it depend on US-origin parts in manufacturing.

EHang said 95% of its 2024 revenue came from the Chinese market and its core business focuses on urban air mobility offerings in that market.

Shares were down more than 11% in recent Friday trading.

data.title: U.S. Stocks Fall Further After China Retaliates Against Trump Tariffs
data.description:
By Vicky Ge Huang

Stocks fell further on Friday after China moved to retaliate against U.S. tariffs, dashing hopes for a speedy resolution to the trade war.

Major indexes opened sharply lower, brushing off a better-than-expected jobs report for March. The economy added 228,000 jobs last month, well above the Wall Street consensus estimate of 140,000.

China said it would impose an additional 34% tariff on all imported U.S. goods, matching President Trump's latest levies on Chinese imports. The extra tariff is set to take effect on April 10.

"Obviously the markets are just simply not taking P's and Q's today from the Labor data whatsoever," said David Bahnsen, chief investment officer of The Bahnsen Group in Newport Beach, Calif. "This is entirely around the China reciprocal tariffs."

Wall Street is set to face another day of significant losses. The S&P 500 dropped 2.9%

The Dow Jones Industrial Average fell 2.7%, and the Nasdaq Composite Index declined 3.2%. The tech-focused Nasdaq is on pace to end the day in a bear market, meaning the benchmark is down 20% from its previous highs.

Investors continued to search for safety in government bonds and gold amid the market turmoil. The yield on the 10-year Treasury, a leading barometer of economic conditions, fell below 4% for the first time since last October, suggesting heightened fears of a recession. Gold slid but held close to record highs.

The declines come after U.S. markets suffered on Thursday their steepest declines since 2020, with investors fearing that President Trump's new tariff plans will trigger a global trade war and drag the U.S. economy into recession.

data.description: The Bank of Canada likely opts for another rate cut on April 16, says Manulife Investment Management economist Dominique Lapointe, citing one of the weakest jobs reports in years and expectations of further job losses in the country's manufacturing sector due to Trump's tariff plan. March labor data points to an "acute" 62,000 drop in full-time employment, and 48,000 decline in private-sector jobs. Lapointe says losses were concentrated in services industries exposed to domestic conditions: retail and wholesale trade, information, culture and recreation. While Canada faces no new duties from President Trump's tariff order, key Canadian sectors--most notably autos--still face hefty tariffs. Lapointe expects further job losses in Canada, and worries about tariff-fueled inflation from BOC officials to soften due to Canada's limited retaliatory response. (Paul.Vieira@wsj.com; @paulvieira)

data.title: GameStop's stock is climbing again. And this is why.
data.description:
By James Rogers

CEO Ryan Cohen ups his stake in the company, sending shares higher in after-hours trading

Shares of GameStop Corp. rose 2.7% in extended trading Thursday after Chief Executive Ryan Cohen increased his stake in the videogame retailer and original meme stock.

On Thursday, Cohen purchased 500,000 shares at a price of $21.55 in the open market, according to an SEC filing. The purchase took Cohen's GameStop (GME) stake to 37,347,842 shares, or approximately 8.4% of the shares outstanding.

The founder and former CEO of online pet-supplies retailer Chewy Inc. (CHWY) was named GameStop's CEO in September 2023, sending its stock soaring. In December 2023, GameStop's board of directors approved a new investment policy permitting the company to invest in equity securities, among other things. The board gave Cohen the authority to manage the investment portfolio.

Related: GameStop finally makes its bitcoin move, and investors seem fired up

After weeks of bitcoin buzz swirling around the company, GameStop recently added bitcoin (BTCUSD) to its investment policy as a treasury-reserve asset. The videogame retailer also announced a proposed private offering of $1.3 billion worth of convertible senior notes. Earlier this week, GameStop said that it had completed the offering, and also exercised an option to purchase up to an additional $200 million aggregate principal amount of notes.

Cohen was in the most recent MarketWatch 50 - which lists the 50 most influential people in the markets.

Shares of GameStop, like AMC Entertainment Holdings Inc. (AMC), skyrocketed in 2021 amid the meme-stock frenzy. However, recent years have been marked by declining sales and intense competition from digital gaming and streaming.

Related: Why GameStop is trying to be the next MicroStrategy with its bitcoin move

Nonetheless, GameStop's stock climbed again last year with the return of influential trader Keith Gill, also known as Roaring Kitty, to social media. Gill, an important figure in the 2021 meme-stock explosion, described himself as a GameStop "believer" in his first YouTube livestream in three years last year.

GameStop shares, which ended Thursday's regular session down 7%, are up 83% in the past 12 months.

-James Rogers

data.title: NextNRG Extends Fueling Services to Sunbelt Rentals in Texas
data.description:
NextNRG said Friday it's expanding its commercial relationship with equipment rental company Sunbelt Rentals by supporting Sunbelt's fueling needs in Texas.

NextNRG said it has set up a fueling portal for Sunbelt to allow authorized users to schedule deliveries, monitor fueling activity, and access data.

The company said its mobile fueling division delivered about 1.44 million gallons in February, up 166% from a year earlier.

data.title: Upwork dips as BTIG cuts to 'neutral' on tariff and AI risks
data.description:
** Shares of freelancing platform Upwork fall 5.5% to $11.85

** Brokerage BTIG downgrades UPWK to "neutral" from "buy"; maintains PT of $18

** Says "fundamentals look primed to take another leg down"

** Brokerage says UPWK relies heavily on international markets, which drove 81% of its 2024 revenue growth

** Says co's 2025 sales could fall 6-7% post tariffs; says co will still maintain "healthy EBITDA levels"

** Economic downturn could accelerate AI replacing freelancers as "nearly 90% of automation-related job losses occur in the first year of a recession" - BTIG

** As of last close, stock down 23.4% YTD

data.title: Oil companies slip, crude prices head lower after China's tariff announcement
data.description:
** Shares of oil companies fell in morning trading following prices of crude

** Brent futures fall 6.8% to $65.39/bbl; U.S. West Texas Intermediate crude futures decline 7.6% to $61.88/bbl by 1341 GMT (9:41 a.m. ET)

** Earlier in the session, WTI and Brent both hit lowest since April 2021

** China, top importer of oil, announced additional tariffs of 34% on U.S. goods, escalating the trade war with President Donald Trump

** Crude also weighed down by hit from Trump's tariffs from Wednesday and output increases announced by the OPEC+ producer group

** Tariffs could lead to slowing down of global economy, which could hit fuel demand world over

** U.S. oil majors Exxon Mobil down 3.5%, while Chevron falls 4.1%, producer Occidental Petroleum down 5.7%

** Oilfield services company Halliburton declines 7.5% and top oilfield co SLB down 5.8%

** Refiner Marathon Petroleum down 5.6%, Valero down 5.3%

data.title: Tesla, Apple Shares Fall After China Imposes Tariffs on US Imports
data.description:
Tesla and Apple stocks dropped 6% and 2.4%, respectively, on Friday following China's new tariffs on US goods.

China plans to impose an extra 34% tariff starting April 10, raising concerns about a possible global recession, Reuters reported.

Beijing will also limit exports of key rare-earth minerals and placed 11 US companies on an "unreliable entity" list, the media outlet reported.

Tesla and Apple earn a large part of their revenue from China, making them highly vulnerable to these actions, according to Reuters.

Other major US tech companies also saw declines. Alphabet fell 0.7%, Microsoft dropped 0.8%, and Meta was down 3.8%.

Tesla, Apple, Alphabet, Microsoft and Meta did not immediately respond to requests for a comment from MT Newswires.

data.title: Darag agrees portfolio transfer for Protector’s Danish workers’ comp portfolio
data.description:
(The Insurer) - Legacy specialist Darag Group has entered into a portfolio transfer agreement with Protector Forsikring for its entire workers’ compensation portfolio in Denmark.

Subject to approval by the Norwegian and German regulators, a Danish workers’ compensation portfolio with around 130 million euros ($143.3 million) of net liabilities will be transferred from Protector to Darag.

The transaction, conducted through Darag Deutschland AG, will allow Protector to fully exit this line of business.

Tom Booth, CEO of Darag, said the deal was the legacy specialist’s 70th transaction across 22 jurisdictions in its 15 years of operation.

The latest deal comes less than a month after Darag signed a loss portfolio transfer agreement with UK-based insurer Soteria Insurance, the former underwriting business of the Co-operative Group.
